text: One of the big ideas I see circulated around this community is the power of spreadsheets. I've used spreadsheets for keeping track of some numbers, but I haven't used spreadsheets in any serious capacity. I have maybe a small sense of what makes spreadsheets compelling, but I suspect I'm missing some things. I'm curious if there are any "canonical" articles out there that describe the core ideas about spreadsheets and their relevance for the future of coding. If not, I'm curious how members of this community came about these ideas, and what we would say those ideas are.

text: Without being technical, it's important to understand the social role.  There is a certain class of people for whom their entire professional life revolves around Excel, Outlook, and to a lesser degree Word.  Excel is their outlining, calculating, charting, and programming environment.  People have told me that, from their business's perspective, Excel is Microsoft's primary product with Windows as the Excel delivery mechanism.

elements.text: Without being technical, it's important to understand the social role.  There is a certain class of people for whom their entire professional life revolves around Excel, Outlook, and to a lesser degree Word.  Excel is their outlining, calculating, charting, and programming environment.  People have told me that, from their business's perspective, Excel is Microsoft's primary product with Windows as the Excel delivery mechanism.

text: Since I’ve been building a commercial “future of coding” style platform, I’ve formed a few opinions on this. IMHO  apart from all the declarative formula based stuff its important to realize one really powerful thing about spreadsheets - they let people just get going inputting data in a free form fashion. There is really no cognitive burden around app design, screen building, metadata notions, any of that stuff. The problem of course is that as a result it is difficult to take the spreadsheet to the next level. Therefore the spreadsheet stays at that limited but very valuable use case - initial data entry, lightweight compute, and some level of collaborative information curation and syndication.

text: And cleaning them up is difficult.  Excel for instance has a lot of mostly hidden state above the level of individual formulas.  So you end up with weird path dependencies where the way you build the sheet so far affects what will happen when you introduce new rows and columns.

elements.text: And cleaning them up is difficult.  Excel for instance has a lot of mostly hidden state above the level of individual formulas.  So you end up with weird path dependencies where the way you build the sheet so far affects what will happen when you introduce new rows and columns.

text: All add too, point back to my first post, that Excel as social network reduces the utility of other spreadsheet, charting, database tools, so lots of people end up kind of stuck in a rut.

text:
What <@U01JNTE35QS> says about spreadsheets also applies to the "computational notebooks" (Jupyter etc.) that are becoming very popular in computational science and data science. For both tool families, the initial barrier to becoming productive is low, but there is no support for managing the evolution (and in particular growth) of the software artefact. Which has as a net result the establishment of bad practices.

The lesson for FoC projects, in particular in the category of "end-user programming" (much as I dislike that term) is that such systems need to consider growth paths from the start. Which means pointing out the limits of a particular interface early on, and facilitating the migration to more complex but also more powerful tools.

text: That should be possible in the long run, if you complement WASM with a library for local resource management. You don't want to lose your software installation when you close your browser. But "possible" is very different from "easy". The hard work behind Guix is not designing and implementing the infrastructure, but patching thousands of software packages to fit into the framework. That's hard enough for today's Guix which is a variant of Linux (same kernel, same basic filesystem (POSIX), but very different conventions for file management). For WASM, it's likely to be worse.

text:
Unison is an open source functional programming language based on a simple idea with big implications: code is content-addressed and immutable.

<https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TZZlt6-bKW8>

text: The solve (or at least I’m hoping is the solve) we have undertaken is to build our system in such a way that the onboarding toolset is itself an app built using our system. And that it facilitates a seamless move into more powerful tooling.

elements.text: The solve (or at least I’m hoping is the solve) we have undertaken is to build our system in such a way that the onboarding toolset is itself an app built using our system. And that it facilitates a seamless move into more powerful tooling.

text: That approach needs a lot of expressive power in the base layer of the system which is what we spent a few years building.

text: I was thinking about Unison the other day, and wondering - if there are several implementations, or even several versions of implementations, running unison, what happens if the same unison code results in a different result?

text: Hello! Most of my career has seen me working in a visual programming language, namely LabVIEW. Because of this experience, when I move to my other favorite languages like F# or Racket, it feels like 2 steps forward and 3 steps backwards, primarily when it comes to being able to debugging and especially while the program is running. I am inspired by visual languages like vvvv, Pure Data, and TouchDesigner, and I am convinced that the future of programming is a hybrid approach, where there is tight integration but blurred lines between what is textually and visually specified. I am learning Pure Data and vvvv at the moment, and still have it on my plate to learn Touch Designer and Pharo, all the while I am working on designing my own language while also learning how to generally do so. :) The trouble is I'm not the smartest tool in the shed, so progress is slow but becoming tangible.
